stem: A `BankAccount` class needs a field `balance` of type `double` that stores a separate balance for each individual account. Which of the following is the proper declaration for this field?

options.label: A
options.text: `public double balance;`

options.label: B
options.text: `private static double balance;`

options.label: C
options.text: `private double balance;`

options.label: D
options.text: `public static double balance;`

explanation: Instance variables should be `private` (encapsulation) and non-`static` so each object has its own copy. `public` exposes internal state to all classes; `static` would share one balance across every `BankAccount` object.

options.label: A
options.text: The instance variable `gradeLevel` is correctly updated to the parameter value.

options.label: B
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because a parameter cannot share a name with an instance variable.

options.label: C
options.text: The instance variable `gradeLevel` is not updated; the assignment stores the parameter into itself.

options.label: D
options.text: A `NullPointerException` is thrown at runtime.

explanation: The parameter `gradeLevel` shadows the instance variable `gradeLevel`. The statement `gradeLevel = gradeLevel` assigns the parameter to itself, leaving the field unchanged. Using `this.gradeLevel = gradeLevel;` would fix the bug.

options.label: A
options.text: `getWidth()` returns `5`; `getIsEmpty()` returns `true`

options.label: B
options.text: `getWidth()` returns `5`; `getIsEmpty()` returns `false`

options.label: C
options.text: `getWidth()` returns `0`; `getIsEmpty()` returns `false`

options.label: D
options.text: `getWidth()` returns `0`; `getIsEmpty()` returns `true`

explanation: The constructor explicitly sets `width = 5`. The field `isEmpty` is never assigned, so it keeps its Java default value of `false` for `boolean`. Distractor A assumes uninitialized booleans default to `true`; distractors C and D assume the constructor never ran.

options.label: A
options.text: The code compiles and runs without error.

options.label: B
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because `count` is `private` and cannot be accessed inside a static method.

options.label: C
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because a non-static variable cannot be referenced from a static context.

options.label: D
options.text: A runtime error occurs because `count` is not initialized before it is assigned.

explanation: `count` is an instance variable belonging to individual objects. A `static` method has no implicit `this` reference and therefore cannot access instance variables. The compiler reports "non-static variable cannot be referenced from a static context." Distractor B confuses the cause — `private` is fine inside the same class; the issue is `static` context.

stem: Which of the following best describes encapsulation as it applies to a Java class?

options.label: A
options.text: Breaking a large method into smaller helper methods to reduce complexity

options.label: B
options.text: Declaring instance variables `private` and providing `public` methods to access or modify them

options.label: C
options.text: Reusing the behavior of one class inside another class without rewriting it

options.label: D
options.text: Ensuring all methods depend only on their parameters and never on stored object state

explanation: Encapsulation hides internal data (`private` fields) and controls access through `public` methods, protecting object state from unintended outside modification. Choice A describes method decomposition, C describes code reuse, and D describes stateless utility methods.

The constructor sets the instance field `this.x = 3`. In `shift(10)`, `this.x = this.x + x = 3 + 10 = 13`. `getX()` returns the instance field, which is 13. Distractor A ignores the shift entirely; B returns only the parameter; D adds `y` instead of `x`.

stem: A programmer wants to write a setter for the `name` field in the `Person` class. Which of the following implementations contains a bug that prevents the instance variable from being updated?

options.label: A
B
C
D

explanation: In option C, the parameter `name` shadows the instance variable `name`. The assignment `name = name` stores the parameter into itself and never touches the field. Options A and D use a distinct parameter name that does not shadow the field; option B correctly uses `this.name` to disambiguate.

Each constructor call increments the shared static `count` first, then assigns `id = count`. So w1.id=1, w2.id=2, w3.id=3, and count=3. The output is `1 3 3`. Distractor C mistakenly assumes `id` reflects the final value of `count` for all objects; D assumes `count` stops incrementing after the first object.

options.label: A
options.text: The code will not compile because `radius` is `private` and cannot be accessed via a parameter reference.

options.label: B
options.text: The code compiles correctly because a method may access the `private` fields of another object of the same class.

options.label: C
options.text: The code compiles, but `other.radius` always evaluates to `0.0` because private fields of a parameter are inaccessible at runtime.

options.label: D
options.text: The code will not compile because `other.radius` must be accessed through a public getter method.

explanation: In Java, access control operates at the class level, not the object level. Any method declared inside `Circle` may directly read or write the `private` fields of any `Circle` object, including one passed as a parameter. This enables efficient comparison methods without requiring getter calls.

The constructor sets the instance field `this.val = 10`. In `adjust(3)`, the parameter `val` starts as 3; after `val = val * 2`, the parameter becomes 6 (the instance field is not touched yet). Then `this.val = this.val + val = 10 + 6 = 16`. Distractor A ignores the doubling (10+3). Distractor C doubles the instance field instead (10*2). Distractor D doubles the instance field and then adds the doubled parameter (20+6).

options.label: C
options.text: A compile-time error occurs

options.label: D
options.text: A `NullPointerException` is thrown at runtime

explanation: In the constructor body, `count = count` assigns the parameter `count` to itself — neither side refers to the instance variable, which is shadowed. The instance variable `count` is never set and retains its default `int` value of 0. The fix is `this.count = count;`. Option A is wrong because the instance field was never assigned. Options C and D are wrong because the code compiles and runs without error.

options.label: A
options.text: The class compiles; `doubleSize()` returns twice the current value of `size`

options.label: B
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because a static method cannot directly reference an instance variable

options.label: C
options.text: The class compiles; `doubleSize()` always returns `0`

options.label: D
options.text: A runtime exception is thrown the first time `doubleSize()` is called

explanation: Static methods belong to the class and have no `this` reference, so they cannot access instance variables. Referencing `size` inside `doubleSize()` produces the compile-time error "non-static variable size cannot be referenced from a static context." Options A and C are wrong because compilation fails. Option D is wrong because the error is caught at compile time, not runtime.

options.label: A
options.text: `20` is printed

options.label: B
options.text: `0` is printed

options.label: C
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because `y` is not in scope at the `println` call

options.label: D
options.text: A runtime exception is thrown because `y` has not been initialized

explanation: The variable `y` is declared inside the `if` block; its scope ends when that block closes. Referencing `y` after the closing brace causes a compile-time error. Option A is wrong because `y` is out of scope. Option B is wrong because local variables have no default values (defaults apply only to instance fields). Option D is wrong because the compiler catches the error before the code ever runs.

options.label: A
options.text: It does not compile because `other.x` and `other.y` are `private` and cannot be accessed outside their own object

options.label: B
options.text: It compiles correctly because a method may access `private` fields of a parameter whose declared type is the same class

options.label: C
options.text: It does not compile because `this.x` and `this.y` cannot be referenced inside an instance method

options.label: D
options.text: It compiles correctly only if `x` and `y` are declared `public`

explanation: In Java, access control is enforced at the class level, not the object level. Any method inside `Point` may read or write the `private` fields of any `Point` object, including one received as a parameter. Option A is incorrect because the access restriction does not apply to same-class parameters. Option C is incorrect because `this` is perfectly valid in instance methods. Option D is incorrect because no visibility change is needed.

options.label: D
options.text: A compile-time error occurs

explanation: Inside the constructor, the parameter names `width` and `height` shadow the instance variables of the same names. The assignments `width = width` and `height = height` each assign a parameter to itself, leaving the instance variables at their default `int` value of 0. Thus `getArea()` returns 0 × 0 = 0. Option C (24) is the result that would be produced if `this.width = width` and `this.height = height` had been written. Option D is wrong because the code compiles without error.

stem: Which of the following best describes **shadowing** as it applies to Java instance variables?

options.label: A
options.text: A `private` instance variable cannot be read or written by any method outside the class.

options.label: B
options.text: A local variable or parameter that has the same name as an instance variable hides the instance variable within that method's scope.

options.label: C
options.text: A `static` variable is hidden from instance methods when both share the same name.

options.label: D
options.text: An instance variable is set to its default value when it is declared without an initializer.

explanation: Shadowing occurs when a local variable or parameter shares a name with an instance variable; inside that method body, the name resolves to the local, not the field. A describes encapsulation/access control. C confuses the static-vs-instance distinction with shadowing. D describes default initialization, an unrelated concept.

options.label: A
options.text: A `String` variable cannot be declared inside an `if` or `else` block.

options.label: B
options.text: The variable `label` is out of scope at the `return` statement because it was declared inside the `if`/`else` blocks.

options.label: C
options.text: The method should be declared `void` because the return value depends on a conditional.

options.label: D
options.text: The two declarations of `label` cause a duplicate-variable compile error.

explanation: Local variables are scoped to the block in which they are declared. Both declarations of `label` exist only inside their respective `if` and `else` blocks and are invisible at the `return` statement, causing a compile error. The fix is to declare `String label;` before the `if`/`else`. A is false—any type may be declared inside a block. C is wrong—returning a value from a conditional branch is perfectly valid. D is wrong—each `label` is in its own separate block, so there is no duplicate.

options.label: A
options.text: The method will not compile because `other.gradeLevel` accesses a `private` field from outside the declaring object.

options.label: B
options.text: The method compiles and works correctly because a method may access `private` fields of any object whose declared type is the same class.

options.label: C
options.text: The method compiles but `other.gradeLevel` always evaluates to `0` at runtime because the field is `private`.

options.label: D
options.text: The method will not compile because `gradeLevel` must be written as `this.gradeLevel` to avoid ambiguity with `other.gradeLevel`.

explanation: Java's access rules are class-level, not object-level: any method inside `Student` may read or write the `private` fields of *any* `Student` object, not just `this`. So `other.gradeLevel` is valid. A is wrong for this reason. C is wrong—`private` affects who may access a field, not its value. D is wrong—`this.gradeLevel` is optional when there is no name ambiguity.

options.label: A
options.text: `count` cannot be incremented inside a constructor because it is `static`.

options.label: B
options.text: `getId()` is a `static` method that attempts to return the non-static instance variable `id`.

options.label: C
options.text: `getCount()` must be declared as an instance method in order to return a `static` variable.

options.label: D
options.text: `this.id` cannot appear in a constructor; `this` is only valid inside regular instance methods.

explanation: `getId()` is declared `static`, so it belongs to the class and has no implicit `this` reference. Accessing the non-static field `id` inside it produces the compile error 'non-static variable id cannot be referenced from a static context.' A is wrong—constructors may freely read and modify static variables. C is wrong—static methods can return static variables without issue. D is wrong—`this` is valid in any instance context, including constructors.
The constructor parameter shadows the instance variable, so `count = count;` leaves the field at its default value of 0. The `add` method correctly uses `this.count`, so the field becomes 3.

stem: A `Book` class should store its title so that outside code cannot directly change the title field. Which declaration best follows encapsulation?

options.label: A
options.text: `public String title;`

options.label: B
options.text: `private String title;`

options.label: C
options.text: `public static String title;`

options.label: D
options.text: `private int title;`

explanation: An instance variable that stores object state should usually be `private`, and a title should be stored as a `String`. The public choices expose the field directly, and `int` is the wrong type for a title.
